# UC-13.1.19 · TCP Output Connection Failures (_internal)

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Advanced &middot; **Pillar:** Platform &middot; **Type:** Fault, Availability &middot; **Wave:** Walk &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We help you know when the private roads your machines use to deliver their journals to the central library are what failed. When those roads get fussy, pages pile up or vanish quietly, and we ring a bell so crews fix the path before anyone assumes the library stayed healthy on its own.*

---

## Description

Detects Splunk-to-Splunk TCP output connection failures and queue pressure seen in TcpOutputProc and tcpout_connections narratives when universal forwarders, heavy forwarders, or intermediate aggregation tiers send cooked streams over splunktcp, typically destination TCP 9997 with optional TLS, toward indexer cluster peers or load-balanced receiving endpoints. Failures arise when indexer hosts are down for patching, certificates near expiry misalign handshakes despite healthy-looking configs, path MTU black holes fragment S2S frames, firewall or NAT policy changes reset idle sessions, autoLB rotates during rolling restarts, sslVersionsForServer on receivers disagrees with forwarder sslVersions, or maxQueueSize and reader speed on indexers create backpressure that closes connections and raises cumulative_dropped in metrics.log. The angle is connection-state telemetry plus S2S handshake behavior, autoLB rotation cadence, and queue blocking versus silent drop outcomes rather than generic console health, search scheduler concurrency, holistic forwarder ping tests, license warnings, index disk quotas, KV replication, deployment class churn alone, end-to-end latency after acceptance, SHC artifact replication, HEC listener health, or certificate inventory timelines that UC-13.1.15 treats as its primary story.

## Value

Platform leaders protect continuity of the ingest pipeline by surfacing tcpout flap bursts before customer indexes go quiet, reducing mean time to detect silent data loss when queues absorb shocks for hours then spill. Site owners gain a prioritized ledger that ties TcpOutputProc reasons to metrics.log depth, host CPU context from introspection, and firewall-modeled Network_Traffic rows so network, PKI, and indexer squads argue from one timeline. Runbooks improve because maintenance windows, production forwarder class labels, and allowed destination sets travel with each alert row, preventing duplicate escalations across teams that otherwise chase the same NAT timeout or autoLB tuning mistake. Fleet hygiene reviews use fifteen minute and one hour rolling counts plus fleet median baselines to spot drift early, preserving forensic completeness for regulated tenants who must prove monitoring covered forwarding paths during incidents.

## Implementation

Enable _internal and _introspection visibility for forwarder classes, model Network_Traffic on management paths that see TCP 9997 decisions, maintain splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv with maint windows and production_uf labels, save platform_uc_13_1_19_tcp_output_connection_failures on a fifteen minute cadence with earliest=-4h@h, route CRITICAL and HIGH to platform forwarding on-call, validate outputs.conf and inputs.conf TLS alignment after changes, and cross-check Splunk Monitoring Console TCP Output dashboards weekly.

## Evidence

Saved search platform_uc_13_1_19_tcp_output_connection_failures on a fifteen minute schedule; Splunk Monitoring Console TCP Output dashboard exports; weekly alert CSV archived to a restricted platform evidence index.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

In a lab environment, stop splunkd on one receiving indexer peer that participates in autoLB for a labeled production_uf test forwarder, confirm TcpOutputProc failures and rising metrics.log tcpout_connections counters within thirty minutes, and verify platform_uc_13_1_19_tcp_output_connection_failures returns CRITICAL or HIGH with non-zero failure_count_15m and actionable recommended_action.

### Negative scenario

After restoring the indexer peer and clearing maintenance flags in splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv, with aligned TLS versions and quiet firewall denies on TCP 9997, confirm severity drops below CRITICAL and HIGH for four consecutive fifteen minute schedules and alert filters emit zero rows when scoped to CRITICAL and HIGH only.

## Detailed Implementation

### Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with Splunk platform SREs, forwarding pipeline engineers, and network security teams that approve firewall changes touching splunktcp listeners on indexers and intermediate heavy forwarders. This use case isolates Splunk-to-Splunk TCP output connection state and queue pressure signals that appear in index=_internal and index=_introspection before business indexes show obvious gaps. Universal forwarders and heavy forwarders send cooked events over outputs.conf tcpout groups toward receiving indexers or intermediate aggregation tiers, commonly on TCP 9997 with optional TLS wrapping the channel. Connection failures surface as TcpOutputProc narratives, tcpout-server stanzas in splunkd logs, and structured metrics.log rows where group=tcpout_connections reports current_size_kb, largest_size, smallest_size, and cumulative_dropped counters that climb when destinations refuse sessions or drain slower than producers fill buffers.

Differentiation is explicit so reviewers do not duplicate sibling gold cards. UC-13.1.1 remains the Monitoring Console posture for fleet dashboards and baseline console health rather than tcpout connection forensics. UC-13.1.2 remains search concurrency and scheduler pressure on search heads, not forwarding queues. UC-13.1.3 remains holistic forwarder connectivity and phonehome narratives where tcpout is one lane among many. UC-13.1.4 remains license stack warnings. UC-13.1.5 and UC-13.1.6 remain index sizing and storage policy stories. UC-13.1.7 remains KV store replication health. UC-13.1.8 remains deployment server distribution and class churn. UC-13.1.9 remains end-to-end ingestion latency budgets after events are accepted. UC-13.1.10 remains search head cluster captain, artifact replication, and deployer bundle timelines. UC-13.1.11 and UC-13.1.12 remain cluster peer replication factor and HEC endpoint health respectively. UC-13.1.15 remains certificate inventory and notAfter proximity across roles rather than live tcpout flap counts. This document centers TcpOutputProc connection transitions, autoLB rotation behavior, sslVersionsForServer handshake mismatches, maxQueueSize backpressure, and metrics.log tcpout_connections depth when indexers close sessions under load.

RBAC and safety: grant the saved search role read access to index=_internal and index=_introspection on search heads that can see forwarder-originated internal logs, plus optional indexes that hold forwarder_health modular input output when you operate that collector. In Splunk Cloud, confirm subscription guidance for searching _internal across the deployment; prefer Monitoring Console service identities over personal administrator tokens. Legal and privacy reviewers should confirm destIp fields representing internal management networks are acceptable in shared indexes.

Configuration posture on forwarders: outputs.conf declares a defaultGroup under tcpout plus one or more tcpout-server stanzas naming host or IP, port, and optional ssl directives. useACK=true raises durability expectations and changes how receivers acknowledge batches; when receivers stop acknowledging because disk or parsing stalls, producers still log stress even when the TCP session remains superficially connected. autoLB=true with autoLBFrequency and forceTimebasedAutoLB influences how often forwarders rotate among peers; aggressive rotation during indexer maintenance can resemble failure storms unless change metadata is present in inventory lookups. useClientSSLCompression affects CPU trade-offs on constrained hosts. Server-side inputs.conf splunktcp or ssl stanzas must align sslVersionsForServer and cipher suites with forwarder sslVersions settings or handshakes fail with explicit TLS alerts logged on both sides.

Keystore and ssl-config-stamp hygiene: when operators rotate server.pem or issuing chains, stamp configuration reload order so TcpOutputProc lines correlate with deliberate reloads rather than mystery mid-day flaps. Pair splunkd certificate warnings with UC-13.1.15 only when the question is expiry or trust chain geometry; this card answers connection churn and queue depth even when certificates still have months of validity.

Deployment server and bundle hygiene: classes that rewrite outputs.conf during indexer migrations can strand forwarders pointing at decommissioned hosts until phonehome applies the next bundle; pair this search with deployment server client logs separately rather than pretending tcpout alone explains class skew. Search head cluster members should not be mistaken for indexers in tcpout destinations; mis-pointed defaultGroup from a golden image can generate perpetual connection refused rows until outputs are corrected.

Risk briefing: a short burst of TcpOutputProc errors during a single indexer rolling restart may be benign when autoLB drains fairly; require rolling counts, maintenance correlation from splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv, and Monitoring Console TCP Output views before executive escalation. Symmetric failures across an entire site more often indicate firewall, path MTU, or NAT timeout policy changes than simultaneous indexer crashes.

Collector posture: ensure heavy forwarders relaying _internal from edge forwarders preserve timestamps and TRUNCATE limits large enough for TcpOutputProc lines. When using intermediate heavy forwarders, remember that their own tcpout toward indexers generates separate internal logs; tag host fields with forwarding roles using props transforms or INGEST_EVAL where supported.

Monitoring Console and Forwarder Management: install the Splunk Monitoring Console app on a dedicated monitoring search head, validate distributed mode shows indexer and forwarder assets, and locate TCP Output dashboards that visualize forwarding errors by destination. Where Forwarder Management is supported, use it to confirm last-applied outputs classes complement splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv; Splunk Cloud administrators should follow cloud-specific guidance for accessing equivalent internal telemetry.

Search head cluster operations: schedule this saved search from a stable monitoring search head so SHC captain handovers do not create artificial gaps in scheduled execution windows that could be mistaken for forwarding silence.

Introspection correlation: index=_introspection sourcetype=splunk_resource_usage carries CPU and memory snapshots for forwarder hosts. Elevated CPU during tcpout failure windows supports local saturation hypotheses distinct from pure network black holes.

Network Traffic data model alignment: accelerate Network_Traffic summaries where host firewall or segment ACL logs map into All_Traffic with dest_port=9997 so the tstats arm can contrast administrative deny or reset actions with Splunk-generated connection narratives during change windows.

Closing Step 1 focus: permissions, outputs and inputs TLS alignment, deployment server awareness, Monitoring Console orientation, Forwarder Management context, SHC scheduling caution, introspection value, and explicit differentiation from sibling platform UCs before any SPL is saved.

### Step 2 — Configure data collection

Lane one ingests index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd from source paths matching splunkd.log where TcpOutputProc logs connection lifecycle lines or where group=tcpout_connections appears in structured splunkd events. Preserve complete _raw text for TcpOutputProc because reason phrases differ across minor versions. When heavy forwarders consolidate _internal from many universal forwarders, retain originating forwarder host keys either through host override discipline or through additional metadata fields your organization standardizes.

Lane two ingests index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd from metrics.log selections where group=tcpout_connections. Field aliases should normalize current_size_kb, largest_size, smallest_size, and cumulative_dropped when collectors emit slightly different key spellings. These metrics explain silent pain where TCP remains up yet queues swell or drops increment.

Lane three ingests index=_introspection sourcetype=splunk_resource_usage for forwarder class hosts. CPU and memory snapshots contextualize failure bursts that coincide with host-level saturation rather than network-only theories.

Lane four prepares accelerated CIM Network_Traffic summaries that include management segment firewall or iptables style logs with dest_port=9997. The tstats arm in the saved search expects datamodel=Network_Traffic with nodename=All_Traffic; if your segment logs are not yet modeled, land a minimal props and transforms mapping first, then enable acceleration.

Lane five optionally captures index=_internal component=cooked lines that reference connection, tcp, queue, or forwarder keywords when TcpOutputProc alone is throttled. Keep this lane narrow to avoid unrelated parsing noise.

Forwarder health modular input: when enabled, land structured forwarder_health documents to a dedicated index and sourcetype agreed by platform operations. This saved search does not require that lane inside multisearch because multisearch breadth is already five arms; use forwarder_health dashboards as a second opinion when TcpOutputProc counts disagree with remote heartbeat narratives.

props.conf guidance: set TRUNCATE high enough for long TcpOutputProc lines, honor TIME_PREFIX and MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD appropriate to splunkd logging, and avoid aggressive LINE_BREAKER rules that split stack-like tcpout messages.

Pre-save validation: run a narrow search over index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd TcpOutputProc earliest=-60m on a known forwarder and confirm events exist. Run index=_internal metrics.log group=tcpout_connections earliest=-60m on the same host and confirm numeric fields populate. Validate index=_introspection sourcetype=splunk_resource_usage earliest=-60m returns CPU fields via rex spot checks.

### Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as platform_uc_13_1_19_tcp_output_connection_failures with a fifteen minute schedule in production after tuning, dispatch.earliest_time=-4h@h, dispatch.latest_time=now, and alert when severity is CRITICAL or HIGH after maintenance correlation. Throttle duplicate host,destPort rows for thirty minutes unless severity escalates. Include recommended_action tokens in pager bodies so on-call engineers open tcpout runbooks without rerunning ad hoc searches.

Pipeline narrative clause by clause: the leading comment macro documents intent and tunables for future authors. multisearch fans five arms in parallel so a silent single lane cannot hide a real incident: splunkd TcpOutputProc and tcpout_connections log lines, metrics.log tcpout_connections numeric stress, introspection CPU samples, Network_Traffic tstats for port 9997 administrative actions, and cooked component hints. Each arm normalizes forwarder_id, dest_ip, dest_pt, lane, is_conn_fail, ssl_hs, conn_mode, queue_kb, drop_evt, cpu_pct, and nt_cnt so unioned rows share a schema.

After multisearch, coalesce() ladders unify host, destIp, and destPort across naming drift. dest_key concatenates host and destination port for stable streamstats partitions. sort orders events for meaningful streamstats calculations.

streamstats time_window=900s computes rolling fifteen minute failure sums for CRITICAL logic while time_window=3600s computes one hour sums for fleet comparisons. eventstats median(failure_count_1h) publishes fleet_median_fail_1h for relative severity context.

join type=left with inputlookup splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv enriches maint windows, owner_team, site, ssl_cert_expires, fwd_class, and allowed_dest_set. scheduled_maint_window drives in_maint classification; tune matching logic to your lookup vocabulary.

case() maps CRITICAL when fifteen minute failures exceed ten outside maintenance for production_uf class, HIGH when ssl_hs indicates TLS handshake or certificate verification stress, MEDIUM for ConnReset style refusal modes, and LOW during maintenance windows.

recommended_action provides terse routing hints rather than replacing runbooks.

Fenced SPL must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.1.19 TCP output S2S failures: TcpOutputProc plus metrics.log tcpout_connections, introspection CPU, Network_Traffic tstats on 9997, cooked hints; inventory join; streamstats 15m and 1h; eventstats median. Tunables: earliest=-4h@h crit_15m=10")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd source=*splunkd.log* earliest=-4h@h latest=now (TERM(TcpOutputProc) OR "group=tcpout_connections" OR group=tcpout_connections OR TERM(tcpout) OR TERM(tcpout-server))
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(destIp,dest_IP,dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt=trim(toString(coalesce(destPort,dest_port,"9997")))
      | eval lm=lower(_raw)
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(match(lm,"connection (failed|refused|reset)|connect failed|broken pipe|timed out|time out|ssl.*handshake|tls.*handshake|certificate verify|alert certificate|unknown ca"),1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=if(match(lm,"ssl|tls") AND match(lm,"handshake|certificate|alert|verify"),1,0)
      | eval conn_mode=case(match(lm,"reset"),"ConnReset", match(lm,"refused"),"ConnRefused", match(lm,"timeout|timed out"),"Timeout", true(),"Other")
      | eval lane="internal_tcpout_splunkd"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,hostname,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ search index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd source=*metrics.log* earliest=-4h@h latest=now group=tcpout_connections
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt=trim(toString(coalesce(destPort,port,"9997")))
      | eval qkb=tonumber(coalesce(current_size_kb,"0"))
      | eval cum_drop=tonumber(coalesce(cumulative_dropped,"0"))
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(cum_drop>0 OR qkb>10240,1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=0
      | eval conn_mode="MetricsQueue"
      | eval lane="metrics_tcpout_queue"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval lm=lower(_raw)
      | eval queue_kb=qkb
      | eval drop_evt=cum_drop
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ search index=_introspection sourcetype=splunk_resource_usage earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | rex field=_raw max_match=1 "(?i)\"cpu_system_pct\"\s*:\s*(?<cpu_sys>[0-9.]+)"
      | eval cpu_pct=tonumber(cpu_sys)
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval dest_ip=""
      | eval dest_pt=""
      | eval lane="intro_res_cpu"
      | eval is_conn_fail=0
      | eval ssl_hs=0
      | eval conn_mode="HostPressure"
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ | tstats summariesonly=t latest(_time) AS _time count AS nt_cnt values(All_Traffic.action) AS nt_actions FROM datamodel=Network_Traffic WHERE nodename=All_Traffic All_Traffic.dest_port=9997 earliest=-4h@h latest=now BY All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest
      | rename "All_Traffic.src" AS src "All_Traffic.dest" AS dest
      | eval lm=lower(mvjoin(nt_actions," "))
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(match(lm,"deny|drop|block|reset"),1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=0
      | eval conn_mode="NT_DM"
      | eval lane="nt_dm_traffic_9997"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(src,dest,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt="9997"
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ search index=_internal source=*splunkd.log* component=cooked earliest=-4h@h latest=now (TERM(connection) OR TERM(tcp) OR TERM(queue) OR TERM(forwarder))
      | eval lm=lower(_raw)
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(match(lm,"fail|refused|reset|stall|close|drop"),1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=if(match(lm,"ssl|tls"),1,0)
      | eval conn_mode=case(match(lm,"reset"),"ConnReset", match(lm,"refused"),"ConnRefused", true(),"CookedOther")
      | eval lane="cooked_tcpout_hint"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(destIp,dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt=trim(toString(coalesce(destPort,"9997")))
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
| eval host=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(forwarder_id, evt_host, ""))))
| eval destIp=trim(toString(coalesce(dest_ip, "")))
| eval destPort=trim(toString(coalesce(dest_pt, "9997")))
| eval dest_key=host."|".destPort
| sort 0 host dest_key - _time
| streamstats time_window=900s global=f sum(eval(if(is_conn_fail==1 AND (lane=="internal_tcpout_splunkd" OR lane=="metrics_tcpout_queue" OR lane=="cooked_tcpout_hint"),1,0))) AS failure_count_15m BY host dest_key
| streamstats time_window=3600s global=f sum(eval(if(is_conn_fail==1 AND (lane=="internal_tcpout_splunkd" OR lane=="metrics_tcpout_queue" OR lane=="cooked_tcpout_hint"),1,0))) AS failure_count_1h BY host dest_key
| eventstats median(failure_count_1h) AS fleet_median_fail_1h
| join type=left max=0 host
    [| inputlookup splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv
      | eval host=lower(trim(toString(host)))
      | fields host,fwd_role,fwd_class,site,ssl_cert_expires,owner_team,scheduled_maint_window,allowed_dest_set ]
| eval in_maint=if(match(lower(trim(toString(coalesce(scheduled_maint_window,"")))),"active|open|true|1|yes|window"),1,0)
| eval severity=case(
    failure_count_15m>10 AND in_maint==0 AND match(lower(trim(toString(coalesce(fwd_class,"")))),"production_uf"),"CRITICAL",
    ssl_hs==1,"HIGH",
    match(conn_mode,"ConnReset"),"MEDIUM",
    in_maint==1,"LOW",
    true(),"MEDIUM")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL","page_fleet_owner_check_indexer_tcpin_ssl_firewall_nat_ack_backlog",
    severity=="HIGH","inspect_tls_chain_sslversionsforserver_and_forwarder_ssl_versions_reload_outputs",
    severity=="MEDIUM","sample_btool_outputs_on_host_verify_autolb_and_maxqueuesize",
    true(),"monitor_mc_tcp_output_panel_and_forwarder_health_lane")
| table _time host destIp destPort lane failure_count_15m failure_count_1h fleet_median_fail_1h severity fwd_class site owner_team ssl_cert_expires in_maint queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt recommended_action
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```ini
[platform_uc_13_1_19_tcp_output_connection_failures_alert]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -4h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch platform_uc_13_1_19_tcp_output_connection_failures | where severity IN ("CRITICAL","HIGH")
```

Performance: if Job Inspector shows multisearch imbalance, materialize each lane hourly into a summary index keyed on host and destPort, widen operational alerts to earliest=-2h@h on summaries, and retain the full search for investigations.

### Step 4 — Validate

Ground truth begins on a representative forwarder and its declared outputs.conf via splunk btool outputs list on the host, comparing tcpout-server host and port to destIp and destPort rows surfaced by the search. When Splunk shows failures toward an IP that btool does not list, suspect intermediate load balancer NAT or DNS rotation rather than parser hallucination.

Controlled outage: in a lab indexer cluster, stop splunkd on one receiving peer that participates in autoLB for a subset of forwarders, run the saved search across a thirty minute window, and confirm failure_count_15m rises for dependent forwarders while peers still up receive traffic. Restore splunkd and confirm counts decay and severity relaxes.

TLS positive control: on a lab forwarder, temporarily misalign sslVersions between forwarder outputs and receiver inputs under vendor-approved change, reload splunkd, and confirm HIGH severity classification with ssl_hs patterns in TcpOutputProc or adjacent SSL component lines. Revert to aligned versions and confirm recovery within two scheduled windows.

Queue narrative: induce controlled send pressure in lab using a deliberately low maxQueueSize on a non-production forwarder class and observe metrics_tcpout_queue lane queue_kb and drop_evt growth correlating with internal_tcpout_splunkd failures. Restore sane queue limits and confirm metrics stabilize.

Monitoring Console cross-check: open TCP Output dashboards in Splunk Monitoring Console and compare visual aggregates to the tabular severities for the same time window; disagreement usually means time skew, host field normalization drift, or different earliest bounds.

Network Traffic cross-check: when CIM arm flags denies or resets, validate firewall change tickets and actual ACL rows on the management path; silent Splunk-only failures without firewall denies point to indexer-side closes or TLS issues.

Negative test: after maintenance completes and all indexers accept test splunktcp connections, confirm filtering severity to CRITICAL and HIGH yields zero rows for four consecutive schedules when inventory marks no maintenance.

Clock skew: validate NTP on forwarders and indexers before blaming TLS; skew can prolong handshake retries that resemble network flaps.

### Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Operationalize paging policy by site and forwarder fleet owner using owner_team from splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv, route CRITICAL pages through platform operations bridges, and reserve HIGH pages for TLS-focused PKI responders. Archive weekly CSV exports of alert results to a restricted evidence index for continuity reviews. Maintain a runbook section covering indexer health checks, certificate timelines distinct from UC-13.1.15 detail panels, firewall ACL verification, NAT timeout tables, splunk btool outputs list sampling, and safe queue draining procedures when cumulative_dropped rises.

Case A — Indexer cluster rolling restart: expect transient TcpOutputProc spikes while autoLB rotates peers; require sustained fifteen minute counts and absence of maintenance flags before treating as incident.

Case B — Expired server certificate on a master forwarder image: receivers reject TLS handshakes; HIGH severity rises with ssl_hs patterns; renew and redeploy bundles before chasing network tickets.

Case C — autoLB misconfiguration: autoLBFrequency too aggressive or forceTimebasedAutoLB mismatch causes benign reconnect storms; tune rotation intervals against vendor guidance and validate with Monitoring Console baselines.

Case D — maxQueueSize too small for steady throughput: metrics_tcpout_queue shows climbing current_size_kb and cumulative_dropped while CPU remains moderate; increase queue limits or add receiving capacity.

Case E — Firewall NAT timeout on idle splunktcp: sessions drop after idle intervals yet immediate reconnect succeeds; adjust middlebox timers or enable keepalive style behaviors consistent with security policy.

Case F — SOCKS or TLS interception proxy intermittently resets connections: failures correlate with proxy maintenance windows; capture pcaps on approved taps and validate SNI and certificate chains through the proxy path.

Case G — S2S protocol downgrade attempt or cipher mismatch: sslVersionsForServer on indexer inputs does not overlap forwarder sslVersions; align configurations and reload both sides under change control.

Case H — Indexer disk full or splunktcp thread starvation: receiver closes connections; correlate with indexer disk usage KPIs and cooked queue warnings distinct from pure network denies.

Case I — Forwarder host clock skew breaking TLS session validation: skew exaggerates handshake retries; fix NTP then retest before reissuing certificates.

Case J — Intermediate universal forwarder chain with wrong defaultGroup: forwarding loops or points at decommissioned peer hostnames; fix outputs.conf classes via deployment server and confirm phonehome success.

Case K — Search head cluster captain handover: scheduled search gaps may pause detection briefly; run from dedicated monitoring search head or verify saved search status across SHC members.

Case L — Load balancer affinity causing benign session resets: sticky sessions flip during backend drain; pair Splunk narratives with load balancer health events before paging application teams.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers are present; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl field exactly; multisearch lists five arms; coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, case, join, inputlookup, multisearch, and tstats appear; final table lists sixteen analyst-visible columns including maintenance and recommended_action.

## SPL

```spl
`comment("UC-13.1.19 TCP output S2S failures: TcpOutputProc plus metrics.log tcpout_connections, introspection CPU, Network_Traffic tstats on 9997, cooked hints; inventory join; streamstats 15m and 1h; eventstats median. Tunables: earliest=-4h@h crit_15m=10")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd source=*splunkd.log* earliest=-4h@h latest=now (TERM(TcpOutputProc) OR "group=tcpout_connections" OR group=tcpout_connections OR TERM(tcpout) OR TERM(tcpout-server))
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(destIp,dest_IP,dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt=trim(toString(coalesce(destPort,dest_port,"9997")))
      | eval lm=lower(_raw)
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(match(lm,"connection (failed|refused|reset)|connect failed|broken pipe|timed out|time out|ssl.*handshake|tls.*handshake|certificate verify|alert certificate|unknown ca"),1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=if(match(lm,"ssl|tls") AND match(lm,"handshake|certificate|alert|verify"),1,0)
      | eval conn_mode=case(match(lm,"reset"),"ConnReset", match(lm,"refused"),"ConnRefused", match(lm,"timeout|timed out"),"Timeout", true(),"Other")
      | eval lane="internal_tcpout_splunkd"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,hostname,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ search index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd source=*metrics.log* earliest=-4h@h latest=now group=tcpout_connections
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt=trim(toString(coalesce(destPort,port,"9997")))
      | eval qkb=tonumber(coalesce(current_size_kb,"0"))
      | eval cum_drop=tonumber(coalesce(cumulative_dropped,"0"))
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(cum_drop>0 OR qkb>10240,1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=0
      | eval conn_mode="MetricsQueue"
      | eval lane="metrics_tcpout_queue"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval lm=lower(_raw)
      | eval queue_kb=qkb
      | eval drop_evt=cum_drop
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ search index=_introspection sourcetype=splunk_resource_usage earliest=-4h@h latest=now
      | rex field=_raw max_match=1 "(?i)\"cpu_system_pct\"\s*:\s*(?<cpu_sys>[0-9.]+)"
      | eval cpu_pct=tonumber(cpu_sys)
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval dest_ip=""
      | eval dest_pt=""
      | eval lane="intro_res_cpu"
      | eval is_conn_fail=0
      | eval ssl_hs=0
      | eval conn_mode="HostPressure"
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ | tstats summariesonly=t latest(_time) AS _time count AS nt_cnt values(All_Traffic.action) AS nt_actions FROM datamodel=Network_Traffic WHERE nodename=All_Traffic All_Traffic.dest_port=9997 earliest=-4h@h latest=now BY All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest
      | rename "All_Traffic.src" AS src "All_Traffic.dest" AS dest
      | eval lm=lower(mvjoin(nt_actions," "))
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(match(lm,"deny|drop|block|reset"),1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=0
      | eval conn_mode="NT_DM"
      | eval lane="nt_dm_traffic_9997"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(src,dest,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt="9997"
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
    [ search index=_internal source=*splunkd.log* component=cooked earliest=-4h@h latest=now (TERM(connection) OR TERM(tcp) OR TERM(queue) OR TERM(forwarder))
      | eval lm=lower(_raw)
      | eval is_conn_fail=if(match(lm,"fail|refused|reset|stall|close|drop"),1,0)
      | eval ssl_hs=if(match(lm,"ssl|tls"),1,0)
      | eval conn_mode=case(match(lm,"reset"),"ConnReset", match(lm,"refused"),"ConnRefused", true(),"CookedOther")
      | eval lane="cooked_tcpout_hint"
      | eval forwarder_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,""))))
      | eval evt_host=forwarder_id
      | eval dest_ip=trim(toString(coalesce(destIp,dest,"")))
      | eval dest_pt=trim(toString(coalesce(destPort,"9997")))
      | eval queue_kb=0.0
      | eval drop_evt=0
      | eval cpu_pct=null()
      | eval nt_cnt=0
      | fields _time evt_host forwarder_id dest_ip dest_pt lane is_conn_fail ssl_hs conn_mode queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt ]
| eval host=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(forwarder_id, evt_host, ""))))
| eval destIp=trim(toString(coalesce(dest_ip, "")))
| eval destPort=trim(toString(coalesce(dest_pt, "9997")))
| eval dest_key=host."|".destPort
| sort 0 host dest_key - _time
| streamstats time_window=900s global=f sum(eval(if(is_conn_fail==1 AND (lane=="internal_tcpout_splunkd" OR lane=="metrics_tcpout_queue" OR lane=="cooked_tcpout_hint"),1,0))) AS failure_count_15m BY host dest_key
| streamstats time_window=3600s global=f sum(eval(if(is_conn_fail==1 AND (lane=="internal_tcpout_splunkd" OR lane=="metrics_tcpout_queue" OR lane=="cooked_tcpout_hint"),1,0))) AS failure_count_1h BY host dest_key
| eventstats median(failure_count_1h) AS fleet_median_fail_1h
| join type=left max=0 host
    [| inputlookup splunk_forwarder_inventory.csv
      | eval host=lower(trim(toString(host)))
      | fields host,fwd_role,fwd_class,site,ssl_cert_expires,owner_team,scheduled_maint_window,allowed_dest_set ]
| eval in_maint=if(match(lower(trim(toString(coalesce(scheduled_maint_window,"")))),"active|open|true|1|yes|window"),1,0)
| eval severity=case(
    failure_count_15m>10 AND in_maint==0 AND match(lower(trim(toString(coalesce(fwd_class,"")))),"production_uf"),"CRITICAL",
    ssl_hs==1,"HIGH",
    match(conn_mode,"ConnReset"),"MEDIUM",
    in_maint==1,"LOW",
    true(),"MEDIUM")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL","page_fleet_owner_check_indexer_tcpin_ssl_firewall_nat_ack_backlog",
    severity=="HIGH","inspect_tls_chain_sslversionsforserver_and_forwarder_ssl_versions_reload_outputs",
    severity=="MEDIUM","sample_btool_outputs_on_host_verify_autolb_and_maxqueuesize",
    true(),"monitor_mc_tcp_output_panel_and_forwarder_health_lane")
| table _time host destIp destPort lane failure_count_15m failure_count_1h fleet_median_fail_1h severity fwd_class site owner_team ssl_cert_expires in_maint queue_kb drop_evt cpu_pct nt_cnt recommended_action
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count FROM datamodel=Network_Traffic WHERE nodename=All_Traffic All_Traffic.dest_port=9997 earliest=-4h@h latest=now BY All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.action
```

## Visualization

Severity-colored table by host and destPort with drilldowns to TcpOutputProc _raw, metrics.log tcpout_connections fields, introspection CPU, Network_Traffic tstats context, and inventory columns; single-value tiles for fleet_median_fail_1h; timeline of failure_count_15m during incidents.

## Known False Positives

Scheduled indexer rolling restarts during approved change windows produce short TcpOutputProc bursts while autoLB drains peers and forwarders reconnect; require sustained fifteen minute counts outside inventory maintenance and Monitoring Console correlation before paging. Planned cluster maintenance with explicit scheduled_maint_window values should downgrade severity to LOW when lookup hygiene is correct. autoLBFrequency misconfigured far too low can create benign reconnect churn that resembles outages; tune against baseline guidance and compare to fleet median before incidents open. Intermediate heavy forwarders moving between indexer peers via DNS rotation generate connection closes that are operationally normal during capacity moves. SOCKS proxies or TLS inspection appliances sometimes reset long-lived splunktcp sessions during their own certificate rotations; pair Splunk timelines with proxy change records. Apple silicon or other forwarder hosts occasionally exhibit kernel networking quirks that produce intermittent TcpOutputProc warnings without indexer faults; validate with host OS patches and controlled pcaps. Queue-full conditions driven by indexer CPU saturation can look like network loss until metrics_tcpout_queue shows depth growth while firewall logs stay quiet; treat as capacity not ACL. Load balancer affinity or drain policies may reset sessions benignly when backends leave pools; correlate with load balancer events. Duplicate _internal ingestion from mis-rotated heavy forwarders can double-count failure rows until dedupe summaries land. Lab forwarders pointed at dark addresses as part of bootstrap testing create refused connections that should never page production bridges. Penetration tests that port-scan indexers may generate firewall deny rows in Network_Traffic without Splunk functional loss; triage with change tickets.
